4 min read
Just now

--

Part 4 of the “API Design Done Right” series. Returning 200 OK with {"success": false} is how you make every client write defensive spaghetti.
Here’s an API response that’s more common than it should be:
HTTP 200 OK
{ "success": false, "error": "User not found" }It returned 200 — the code for success — to report a failure. Now every client has to ignore the HTTP status entirely and dig into the body to find out if anything actually worked. Multiply that across a hundred endpoints with inconsistent error shapes, and every integration becomes a tangle of defensive parsing. How your API communicates failure shapes the client's code as much as how it communicates success — and most APIs get it wrong.
The good news: HTTP already gives you a rich, standard vocabulary for outcomes. Use it correctly and clients can handle errors generically. Here’s how.
An HTTP status code is the first thing every client checks. If you lie to it — 200 for failures, 500 for bad input — you force every client to distrust it and parse your prose instead. Tell the truth in the status code.
Use the Status Code Categories Correctly
HTTP status codes are grouped, and the group alone tells a client how to react:
2xx Success → it worked
4xx Client error → YOU (the caller) did something wrong; don't retry as-is
5xx Server error → WE (the server) failed; retrying might helpThat 4xx-vs-5xx distinction is crucial: it tells the client whose fault it is and whether retrying could help. Returning 500 for a validation error tells the client to retry a request that will never succeed; returning 200 for a server failure hides a real outage. Get the category right first.
The Codes You Actually Need
You don’t need all 60+ codes. A solid REST API uses a small, correct set:
200 OK → success (GET, PATCH, PUT)
201 Created → success, a resource was created (POST)
204 No Content → success, nothing to return (DELETE)
400 Bad Request → malformed input / validation failed
401 Unauthorized → not authenticated (who are you?) [see the auth article]
403 Forbidden → authenticated but not allowed
404 Not Found → the resource doesn't exist
409 Conflict → conflicts with current state (e.g. duplicate)
422 Unprocessable → syntactically fine but semantically invalid
429 Too Many Requests → rate limited [see the rate-limiting article]
500 Internal Error → something broke on our side
503 Service Unavailable → temporarily down / overloadedMaster these dozen and you can express almost any outcome precisely. Using the right one lets clients branch on the status code alone — 401 → redirect to login, 429 → back off and retry, 400 → show the user their input error.
Fix #1: Return a Consistent, Structured Error Body
The status code says what kind of error; the body says the specifics. Make every error across your API share one shape so clients can parse it generically:
{
"error": {
"code": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
"message": "The email address is invalid.",
"details": [
{ "field": "email", "issue": "must be a valid email address" }
]
}
}
• A machine-readable code (VALIDATION_ERROR, INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS) clients can switch on — never make them string-match your human message, which you'll reword someday.

• A human-readable message for logs and developers.

• details for field-level validation so a form can highlight exactly what's wrong.
One consistent error shape across every endpoint is a massive quality-of-life gift to everyone integrating with you. (The RFC 7807 / problem+json standard is a ready-made version of this if you want one.)
Fix #2: Be Specific and Actionable, Safely
Good error messages tell the client what to do, without leaking internals:
❌ "Error" (useless)
❌ "Exception in OrderService line 142: (leaks internals — a security risk)
NullPointerException..."
✅ "Order #4821 cannot be cancelled because (specific + actionable)
it has already shipped."Be helpful to legitimate callers, but never expose stack traces, SQL, or internal paths in API responses — that’s both unprofessional and a security leak (the kind attackers love). Log the gory details server-side; return a clean, useful message to the client.
Fix #3: Don’t Leak, Don’t Lie
Two final guardrails:

• Don’t reveal existence through errors where it matters: for sensitive resources, returning 404 instead of 403 can avoid confirming that something exists to someone not allowed to see it.

• Don’t return 200 for errors, ever. If it failed, the status code must say so. The {"success": false} with 200 anti-pattern breaks every generic HTTP client and every monitoring tool that counts non-2xx responses.
Putting It Together

• Right category — 2xx success, 4xx caller’s fault, 5xx your fault.

• Right specific code — master the dozen that matter; let clients branch on status.

• Consistent structured body — machine code, human message, field details.

• Specific but safe — actionable messages, no leaked internals, never 200 for failure.
The Takeaway
How your API fails is part of its interface, not an afterthought — and clients spend a surprising amount of code reacting to your errors. Done badly (lying status codes, inconsistent shapes, useless messages), you force every integrator into defensive parsing and guesswork. Done right, you lean on HTTP’s existing vocabulary: the correct status code tells clients what happened and whether to retry, and a single consistent error body with a machine-readable code lets them handle every failure generically. Tell the truth in the status line, keep one error shape everywhere, and be specific without leaking — and your API becomes one developers trust even when things go wrong.

This is Part 4 of the API Design Done Right series. Next up: REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC — choosing your API style honestly instead of by hype.
What’s the most confusing API error response you’ve ever had to debug? 👇
